MKT10009 Marketing and the Consumer ExperienceLecture 5 - Marketing analytics and artificial intelligence: Gaining customer insightsPart 3 - Analysing and using marketing information & Other marketing information considerations
Chapter 4 from our Textbook: Principles of Marketing,
8th edition
Presented by Dr Gordon Campbell

Learning Objectives for Lecture 5
4.4 Explain how companies analyse and use marketing information.
4.5 Discuss the special issues some marketing researchers face, including public polity and ethics issues.

Part 3  - Analysing and using marketing information & Other marketing information considerations

Analysing and using marketing information (1 of 2)
Customer relationship management
Customer relationship management (CRM) involves managing detailed information about individual customers and carefully managing customer touch points in order to maximise customer loyalty.
CRM integrates everything that a company’s sales, service and marketing teams know about individual customers, providing a 360-degree view of the customer relationship.
CRM analysts develop data warehouses and use sophisticated data mining techniques to unearth the riches hidden in customer data.

Analysing and using marketing information (2 of 2)
Customer relationship management (cont.)
Uses and benefits of customer relationship management include the following:
integrate customer information
ensure customer information is centralised and accessible
provide deep insights for marketing decisions.
Q: What are some other uses and benefits of CRM?

Other marketing information considerations (1 of 4)
Marketing research in small businesses and not-for-profit organisations
Small businesses and not-for-profit organisations can obtain good marketing insights through observation, formal surveys or secondary data.
Many associations, local media and government agencies provide special help, e.g. VECCI (Victorian Employers’ Chamber of Commerce and Industry), state governments’ Small Business Development Corporations.

Other marketing information considerations (2 of 4)
International marketing organisations
International researchers follow the same steps as domestic researchers but often face more and different problems, such as:
dealing with diverse markets in many different countries
cultural differences
difficulty both in finding good secondary data and in analysing it.
Some of the largest research services organisations operate on a worldwide scale. Nielsen has offices in more than 100 countries.
Q: What are some other problems faced by international researchers compared to domestic researchers?
Q: What are some other problems faced by international researchers compared to domestic researchers?

Other marketing information considerations (3 of 4)
Public policy and ethics in marketing research
Increasing consumer privacy concerns have become a major problem for the marketing research industry.
Researchers should ask only for the information they need, use it responsibly to provide customer value and avoid sharing information without the customer’s permission.
Each company must accept responsibility for policing the conduct and reporting of its own marketing research.

Other marketing information considerations (4 of 4)
Public policy and ethics in marketing research (cont.)
The marketing research industry is considering several options for responding to this problem:
codes of practice
standards
other initiatives.
The Australian Market and Social Research Society (AMSRS) maintains its code of practice in the light of strengthened privacy legislation and other factors.

Analysing and using marketing information
Q: What are some other problems faced by international researchers compared to domestic researchers?
Q: What are some other uses and benefits of CRM?
A:Gathering additional market intelligence.
A: International researchers need to learn about the local culture and local market and competitive conditions before they can effectively research a foreign market. In addition, all questions must be re-phrased using the local language and terminology. For example, survey questions for the British or American markets need to be re-phrased for both Australian English and Australian market conditions. Translations into foreign languages present even more delicate issues.
